questions.prompt: Your assistant's v1 design has 28 tools across 6 connectors. Pre-launch review trims it. What's the right trimming heuristic?

choices.text: Keep them all — flexibility is good.

For each tool, ask: does the v1 JTBD genuinely need it? If not, cut. Default v1 to read-heavy / write-light / no-destructive. Each tool you ship has eval needs, failure modes, prompt cost, and approval-gate decisions; surface area is risk.
Use Opus on all of them.

questions.explanation: v1 surface area should be ruthlessly cut. Read-heavy gives users value with low risk; write tools come after trust; destructive tools wait for proven UX patterns. 'While we're at it' tools are how surface area balloons silently.

questions.prompt: When inventorying assistant tools, what's often forgotten?

choices.text: Internal tools — memory writes (notes about the user), scheduled task management (create/pause/disable schedules), approval-queue management. These are first-class tools the assistant uses to manage itself; they need the same inventory treatment as Gmail or Calendar tools.

questions.explanation: Internal management tools (memory, schedule, approvals) are often built ad-hoc and not inventoried like external tools. Treat them as first-class: name, description, schema, reversibility, trust level. They affect the assistant's behavior just as much.
